access.entityLabel: Walter W. Skeat, The Romans of Partenay, revised 1899 edition

bibliographic.citation: The Romans of Partenay or of Lusignen, otherwise known as The Tale of Melusine, translated from the French of La Coudrette (about 1500–1520 A.D.), edited from a unique manuscript in the Library of Trinity College, Cambridge, with an introduction, notes, and glossarial index, by Walter W. Skeat, EETS Original Series 22 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1866; revised and reprinted 1899).
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 22
bibliographic.title: The Romans of Partenay or of Lusignen, otherwise known as The Tale of Melusine

entities.description: Skeat's EETS edition first published in 1866 and revised and reprinted in 1899. It presents a near-facsimile transcription of R.3.17, extensive editorial punctuation and apparatus, and a bilingual editorial replacement for the leaf missing after line 4550.
entities.label: Walter W. Skeat, The Romans of Partenay, revised 1899 edition
notes: The title page retains 1866 and adds '[Revised and reprinted 1899.]'.
The exact inventory of changes from 1866 to 1899 was not established; the volume itself contains explicit 1899 additions.

rights.basis: The 1899 publication is beyond the ordinary U.S. copyright term; this conclusion is separate from provider conditions on a particular scan.
rights.component: 1899 editorial text, typography, and underlying medieval texts

entities.description: The unique surviving manuscript witness of the Middle English Romans of Partenay. The fifteenth-century paper codex is imperfect at the beginning because f.1 is wanting and has a second lacuna where f.88 is wanting; the surviving poem runs from f.2r through f.124v.
entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17

physicalDescription.extent: 123 folios; f.1 and f.88 are wanting
notes: The catalog reports four seven-line stanzas to a page.
The surviving text begins imperfectly on f.2r and ends on f.124v.

rights.basis: The text predates modern copyright, and U-M marks the source materials public domain. This does not determine rights in modern photographs, editions, transcriptions, or platform files.
rights.component: Fifteenth-century Middle English text as an underlying historical work

entities.description: A fifteenth-century French paper manuscript containing Coudrette's Roman de Mélusine or Roman de Parthenay on fols. 1–148, followed by an anonymous French chronicle on fols. 149r–161v. Skeat used it constantly as a comparison manuscript and took the French replacement for R.3.17's missing folio 88 from Ll.2.5 f.103v onward.

holding.notes: From the library of John Moore; acquired by Cambridge University Library in 1715.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ll.2.5

physicalDescription.extent: At least 161 folios; Coudrette text on fols. 1–148
notes: The exact endpoint and quire-level extent should be checked against a full institutional catalog record before treating the Biblissima summary as a complete codicological description.

entities.description: Peer-reviewed scholarship on the Middle English translation's form, possible context, translator, and relationship to Liber Proverbiorum. The author argues that shared form and diction make common translation authorship highly likely while treating Margaret of Anjou as a plausible but unprovable patron.

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is the anonymous Middle English translation/adaptation, not Coudrette's French source, either manuscript, Skeat's edition, or the U-M encoding.
The 57 French lines and 57 modern English editorial lines printed for the lost folio 88 passage are edition-level supply streams, not languages of the surviving Middle English work.
Skeat's repeated consultation of Ll.2.5 establishes an editorial comparison source, not the physical exemplar used by the medieval translator.

The complete R.3.17 cache contains all 258 canvases exposed by Trinity, from front cover through back cover; the work occupies f.2r–124v, canvases 9–252. The provider exposes no separate spine or edge photographs.
Textual lineation, manuscript foliation, printed pagination, PDF pages, and IIIF canvas positions are kept as separate locator systems.

lineage.summary: CME00045 is the General Corpus XML representation of the anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal Romans of Partenay. The repository file copies the University of Michigan encoding, which selects Skeat's revised 1899 EETS text. Skeat's edition transcribes the unique English witness, Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17, while consulting Cambridge University Library MS Ll.2.5, a French Coudrette manuscript, for punctuation, interpretation, and the passage lost with R.3.17 folio 88. This graph distinguishes work, witnesses, editions, encoding, repository artifact, and digital surrogates. Complete publicly exposed objects are cached for R.3.17 and both Skeat editions; only a selected f.1r image was publicly found for Ll.2.5, and the dated whole-object negative is explicit.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalogSummary.date: Probably c. 1500; unique witness late fifteenth century or c. 1500
catalogSummary.title: The Romans of Partenay

catalogingSubject.label: The Romans of Partenay
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal translation and adaptation of Coudrette's French Roman de Melusine or Roman de Parthenay. It is distinct from Coudrette's source work, the unique surviving English witness Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17, Cambridge University Library MS Ll.2.5 as Skeat's French comparison manuscript, Skeat's 1866 and revised 1899 editions, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, and their digital reproductions.

contentParts.label: Surviving translator's prologue

locators.value: begins f.2r; preceding f.1 is wanting

contentParts.summary: The translator reflects on French, metre, prior authorities, pagan invocation, patronage, and his intention to translate Coudrette's romance.

contentParts.label: Translated romance narrative

locators.value: f.5v through f.121v, with f.88 wanting
French supply begins f.103v for the R.3.17 f.88 gap

notes: The 6,377 metric counts surviving Middle English lines only. The numbered range contains the 56-line physical lacuna, represented editorially by 57 French and 57 modern English lines.
contentParts.summary: The main Melusine and Lusignan narrative through the point where Skeat notes that the Cambridge copy of the French romance ends.

contentParts.label: Concluding litany
contentParts.summary: A prayer for the line of Partenay invoking the Trinity, Virgin, angels, apostles, martyrs, confessors, and saints.

contentParts.label: Translator's epilogue
contentParts.summary: Ten stanzas describing the translator's fidelity, rearrangement of words for metre, relation of French and English line lengths, and appeal for the patron's indulgence.

assertion.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance gives c. 1500. Nuttall places the poem's language and patronage possibilities in a mid-fifteenth-century context but does not establish an alternative exact date; no numeric range is normalized.

dateStatements.display: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17, late fifteenth or around 1500
notes: The witness date is not automatically the translation date; the current Trinity catalog gives the fifteenth century and DMeR gives c. 1500.

extent.description: The XML contains 6,769 surviving Middle English L elements in 967 rhyme-royal LG elements: a 210-line surviving prologue and 6,559 extant lines in the separately numbered poem. The poem's lineation runs 1–6,615, but lines 4,551–4,606 are absent with folio 88; canonical lineation therefore exceeds surviving Middle English by 56 lines. Skeat inserts 57 French L elements and 57 modern English L elements for that gap, producing a raw XML total of 6,883 L and 969 LG. The XML has 229 PB elements but only 227 unique REF values: one title-page image plus 226 numbered edition pages, with pp. 158–159 duplicated across the parallel editorial supply streams. It also has 1,877 marginal and 343 footnote NOTE elements.

formStatements.description: Narrative verse in seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas, normally decasyllabic and rhyming ababbcc; Skeat's prose-like modern English line rendering of the missing folio is editorial matter.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML's 57 French and 57 modern English editorial supply lines represent Skeat's replacement for missing folio 88 and do not make the medieval work trilingual.

Coudrette is source author, not author of the Middle English translation. The University of Michigan item inherits a generic author heading for Couldrette that must not erase the anonymous translator.

Nuttall's common-translator argument linking this poem with Liber Proverbiorum is recorded as a supported hypothesis. Margaret of Anjou is a plausible but unproved patron because the identifying opening leaf is lost.

Skeat's outdated suggestion that the translator might have been born of English parents in Poitou is not promoted into a place-of-composition assertion; the current DMeR question-mark localization is retained with low confidence.

The complete Trinity R.3.17 digital surrogate is a reproduction layer. Its CC BY-NC image license and Trinity's publication-permission policy do not govern the underlying fifteenth-century text or authorize commercial/POD reuse of the images.

next_steps: Review current textual, linguistic, codicological, and patronage scholarship after Nuttall 2016.
Keep the c. 1500 catalog date, mid-fifteenth-century contextual argument, common-translator hypothesis, and Margaret attribution as separate claims.
openQuestions.question: Who translated The Romans of Partenay, when was it translated, was the same person responsible for Liber Proverbiorum, and was Margaret of Anjou or another noble patron the commissioner?

next_steps: Consult current LALME/eLALME, rhyme, and scribal-language studies and distinguish authorial from witness localization.
openQuestions.question: Can the English translation's dialect or place of composition be localized more securely than DMeR's 'Northeast Midlands?' without transferring the surviving scribe's language to the translator?

next_steps: Obtain a complete reproduction of CUL Ll.2.5 and collate f.103v and following with Skeat pp. 158–159.

assertion.notes: The source itself prints a question mark. This is not converted into a firm authorial location and remains distinct from Trinity R.3.17's copying history.

notes: This author statement names the anonymous creator of the Middle English translation-adaptation; Coudrette's authorship belongs to the distinct French source work.

scope.description: Authorship of the French verse source work translated into Middle English

notes: The translator says that French was not his native language and discusses the demands of rendering Coudrette's octosyllabic French into English rhyme royal. His name is not preserved because the opening leaf is lost.
The epilogue describes near line-by-line translation but also word-order changes and compression or expansion required by metre.

scope.description: Modern English editorial rendering printed beside the French supply for the missing folio 88 interval

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal translation and adaptation of Coudrette's French verse romance. The fairy Melusine marries Raymond of Poitiers on condition that he never seeks to see her on Saturdays, builds his power and dynasty, and bears sons whose adventures extend the Lusignan lineage across Europe and the eastern Mediterranean. Raymond's breach of secrecy exposes Melusine's serpentine transformation and separates the couple; later quests, combats, family catastrophes, dynastic praise, prayer, and the translator's reflections on poetic translation bring the poem to its close.

The work survives uniquely in Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17. Folio 1, containing the original opening and perhaps information about the anonymous translator and patron, is lost. Folio 88 is also lost; Skeat replaces its 56-line Middle English interval with 57 French lines from CUL MS Ll.2.5 and a 57-line modern English rendering. These editorial supplies are represented in the XML but are not surviving Middle English text.

notes: This widely used discovery title does not identify the Middle English work with Jean d'Arras's distinct prose Melusine.

notes: This is the surviving incipit on f.2r; folio 1 and therefore the original opening are wanting.
titles.value: For full fayne I wold do that myght you please

notes: The manuscript and edition end with this rubric after Middle English line 6615.

work.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
work.dateDisplay: Probably c. 1500; unique witness late fifteenth century or c. 1500

publication.embeddedTitle: The Romans of Partenay, or of Lusignen: OTHERWISE KNOWN AS The Tale of Melusine: Translated from the French of La Coudrette (before 1500 A.D.)

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal translation and adaptation of Coudrette's French verse romance. The fairy Melusine marries Raymond of Poitiers on condition that he never seeks to see her on Saturdays, builds his power and dynasty, and bears sons whose adventures extend the Lusignan lineage across Europe and the eastern Mediterranean. Raymond's breach of secrecy exposes Melusine's serpentine transformation and separates the couple; later quests, combats, family catastrophes, dynastic praise, prayer, and the translator's reflections on poetic translation bring the poem to its close.
